Cornea.name: Cornea
Cornea.function: The clear, curved front window of the eye. It does about two-thirds of the eye's focusing by bending incoming light, before the lens fine-tunes it. Has no blood vessels — it takes oxygen straight from the air, which is why it can be transplanted so successfully.

Iris.name: Iris
Iris.function: The coloured ring of muscle around the pupil. It opens and closes the pupil to control how much light enters — constricting in bright light and widening in the dark. Its pigment gives your eyes their colour.

Lens.name: Lens
Lens.function: A flexible, transparent disc behind the iris. The ciliary muscle changes its shape to fine-focus light onto the retina — fat for near objects, thin for far ones. It stiffens with age, which is why reading glasses become necessary.

Anterior segment of eyeball.name: Anterior Segment
Anterior segment of eyeball.function: The front chamber between the cornea and the lens, filled with clear aqueous humour. This fluid nourishes the cornea and lens and keeps the eye pressurised and round. Poor drainage here raises eye pressure — the basis of glaucoma.

Posterior segment of eyeball.name: Posterior Segment (vitreous & retina)
Posterior segment of eyeball.function: The large back chamber of the eye, filled with jelly-like vitreous humour and lined by the retina. The retina's photoreceptors turn focused light into nerve signals that travel down the optic nerve to the brain's visual cortex.

Lateral rectus muscle.name: Lateral Rectus
Lateral rectus muscle.function: Pulls the eye outward, away from the nose (abduction). One of the six muscles that aim the eye; controlled by the abducens nerve.

Medial rectus muscle.name: Medial Rectus
Medial rectus muscle.function: Pulls the eye inward, toward the nose (adduction). The strongest of the eye muscles — it does most of the work when both eyes converge to look at something close.

Superior rectus muscle.name: Superior Rectus
Superior rectus muscle.function: Mainly rolls the eye upward (elevation). Works with the inferior oblique to direct your gaze up.

Inferior rectus muscle.name: Inferior Rectus
Inferior rectus muscle.function: Mainly rolls the eye downward (depression). Works with the superior oblique to direct your gaze down.

Superior oblique muscle.name: Superior Oblique
Superior oblique muscle.function: Loops through a pulley (the trochlea) and rotates the eye, helping it look down and inward. Controlled by the trochlear nerve, the only muscle it serves.

Inferior oblique muscle.name: Inferior Oblique
Inferior oblique muscle.function: Rotates the eye to look up and outward. Together the two obliques add the twisting movements that keep your view level as your head tilts.

Levator palpebrae superioris.name: Levator Palpebrae
Levator palpebrae superioris.function: Raises the upper eyelid to keep the eye open. Not an eye-mover itself, but essential to vision — when it fails, the lid droops (ptosis).
